look_at.text: A shelf of business books. The titles blur into one another: 'Radical Ownership', 'The Seven Habits', 'Who Moved My Cheese?' All spine-out, unchipped, unread. Props for a performance.

use.text: I don't need to read any of these. I need to leave.

look_at.text: Bob looks like a man who has accepted his fate gracefully. He's waiting for the document.
Bob glances at your hand. He can see you have something. 'Counter-signature. L10 or above. Don't look at me — I'm only an L8.'
Bob is marginally calmer. 'You found it. Now sign it. You'll need a pen.'
Bob squeezes the stress ball. He's composed enough to be useful. 'Code's 2847. Cabinet's on your left.'
Bob squeezes the stress ball rhythmically. He's visibly more composed. Something approaching human.
Bob. Senior Director, Amazon HR. He has the look of a man who has processed a hundred of these and still finds each one personally expensive.

effects.text: Bob raises an eyebrow.
You have the co-signed document?
He extends his hand.
Signed — good. But you need an L10 or above counter-signature to make it binding. Through the standard process, that's three to five months.
He pauses.
Unless you happen to know someone very, very senior.
Sign it. You need a pen — there might be one in that package, if you can sort it out.
Cabinet code is 2847. That's my cost centre. Please don't tell anyone I told you that.
The MSA is in the grey filing cabinet. The access code is my cost centre number. I can't remember it — it'll be on my computer, but the personnel system needs a USB access dongle. Security protocol.
I can't do anything right now. I'm too stressed to even look at a document. I need a moment.
He doesn't elaborate on what kind of moment.

pick_up.text: I'm not picking up Bob. Not even in a metaphorical sense.

effects.text: Bob looks at it. Then at you. Then back at it. He takes it.
Where did you get this?
He squeezes it once. Some tension leaves his face.
Fine. Sit down.

combinations.text: Bob takes the stress ball. He squeezes it. Something in him unclenches slightly.

effects.text: Bob takes it.
Chen. They were let go on Tuesday. This has been sitting undelivered.
He opens it. Pauses. Reaches inside.
There's a pen in here. Decent one.
He holds it out.
Take it. You'll need it.
You set the package on Bob's desk. He looks at the label.
Chen's. Let me deal with this.
He opens it. There's a pen inside — a good one. He slides it across to you.
Yours.
Bob looks it over.
You've signed it. Good. But to be binding, it needs a counter-signature from an L10 or above. Standard protocol. Through proper channels, we're talking three to five months.
He hands it back.
Unless you have access to someone at that level.
Bob glances at it.
Signed. But it needs an L10 counter-signature. Three to five months the normal way.
He shrugs.
Or not.
Bob takes the document. He reads it — actually reads it — line by line. He reaches the bottom. He looks up.
This is in order. Everything checks out.
He initials the final page.
Alex. You're free. I hope it's better out there than it was in here.
You hand Bob the co-signed agreement. He reviews it carefully, nods, and adds his initials.
You're done here, Alex. Good luck.
It needs a co-signature. You know whose.
He taps the business card column on the document.
The credential has to be verified.
You haven't signed it yet. And it needs to be co-signed. You know the process.
He says this without irony.
Bob looks at the Jeff Bezos business card for a long time.
Where did you get this.
It isn't a question. He doesn't take it.

look_at.text: A small foam stress ball sitting on the desk. It's been squeezed so many times it has a permanent handprint impression. Someone needs it.

pick_up.text: That's on Bob's desk. I'm not taking it while he's sitting right there.

use.text: It's on Bob's desk. Not mine.

look_at.text: The screen shows Bob's HR personnel files. You already got what you needed — cost centre 2847.
Bob's HR system. The personnel files are locked behind a USB access protocol. There's a port on the front of the machine. If I had the right USB device, I could get Bob's cost centre number.
Bob's computer. The screen is on but faced away slightly. He'd notice if I touched it.

use.text: Already done. Cost centre 2847 — the cabinet code.
I need to plug the USB access dongle into this.
Bob's not going to let me touch his computer right now.

combinations.text: You slot in the USB. The personnel system unlocks. You scan for Bob's cost centre. There it is: 2847. That's the filing cabinet code.
Bob doesn't look up. 'Put that away.'

look_at.text: 'BOB JOHNSON — Senior Director, HR'. The font is authoritative. The weight of it lands differently now that you're here in person.

use.text: It's a nameplate. I just need to talk to the man it belongs to.

look_at.text: The filing cabinet, open and ransacked. You got what you came for.
A grey filing cabinet with a keypad lock. You know the code — 2847.
A grey filing cabinet with a keypad lock on the front. I'd need the access code to open it.

open.text: You've already retrieved the separation agreement.
You tap in 2847. The lock clicks. Inside: stacked HR forms, a broken stapler, and at the very back, a thick sealed envelope labelled 'SEPARATION AGREEMENT — Alex'. You take it.
There's a keypad lock on this. I need the access code.
You head back to the HR floor.

use.text: You head back to the HR floor.
